The other strategies below are for those who want to customise their exposure further — they remain simple, but require a minimum of thought and monitoring.
strategiesPage.returns_disclosure_intro: Historical returns indicate the past performance of an index over a given period. They are useful for comparing strategies, but should be interpreted with caution for two important reasons:
strategiesPage.returns_disclosure_high: A high historical return may mean the underlying asset is of high quality — but also that it is currently overvalued, which could lead to lower returns in the future. This is notably the case for US and technology stocks, which have strongly outperformed over the past 20 years.
strategiesPage.returns_disclosure_low: A low historical return may mean the asset has gone through a difficult period — but also that it is currently undervalued and potentially positioned for a rebound. This is an argument often made for emerging and European markets.
strategiesPage.returns_disclosure_summary: In summary: past performance is not indicative of future results. Use historical returns as a point of comparison, not as a guarantee.

brokers.intro_market: The Belgian brokerage market has changed profoundly in recent years. Historically dominated by traditional banks charging high fees (€7–15 per transaction), the market has been transformed by the arrival of online brokers. Since August 2025, MeDirect has taken a further step by making ETFs entirely free, forcing competitors to revise their pricing. Today, it is possible to invest in ETFs at low cost regardless of the broker chosen — the difference now lies more in tax automation, platform quality, and automatic savings features.

questions.hero_subtitle: Real answers to common misconceptions about investing.
questions.footer_suggest: Have a question that isn't here?
questions.footer_disclaimer: These answers are provided for educational purposes only and do not constitute investment advice.
questions.r1_q: I've always heard you can lose everything on the stock market — is that true?
questions.r1_p1: Losing everything is possible if you invest in a single company that goes bankrupt — Enron, Lehman Brothers, or a startup that disappears overnight.
questions.r1_p2: That is precisely why a global ETF like IMIE or IWDA is structurally different. You hold fractions of thousands of companies spread across some twenty developed and emerging countries. To lose everything, the entire global economy would have to collapse simultaneously and permanently — a scenario in which your savings account, property, and pension savings would probably also be worthless.
questions.r1_p3: What you can lose, however, is part of your capital temporarily. In 2008, markets fell by ~50%. Depending on the index and currency, nominal recovery took between 3 and 5 years. In 2020, -35% in March, +70% over the year. Volatility is real — but it is not the same as permanent loss.
questions.r1_p4: The practical rule: only invest money you will not need in the next 5 years.
questions.r2_q: What if I start investing just before a crash?
questions.r2_p1: This is the most common fear — and the data shows it is largely overestimated.
questions.r2_p2: Imagine you had invested 10 000 € on 1 January 2008, just before the worst financial crisis since 1929. In 2024, despite the 2008 crash, the European debt crisis, COVID, and the inflation of 2022, your investment would be worth approximately 35 000 € to 40 000 €.
questions.r2_p3: First, if you invest gradually (e.g. 200 €/month), a crash is actually an opportunity: you buy more shares at the same price. This is what is called dollar-cost averaging.
questions.r2_p4: Second, diversified global markets have always eventually recovered over the long term. Not always quickly — sometimes in 3 years, sometimes in 10 — but over a long horizon, the trend is structurally upward. (Some local markets like Japan took several decades — all the more reason to diversify globally rather than betting on a single country.)
questions.r2_p5: The real question is not 'is now the right time?' but 'do I have a long enough horizon?'
questions.r3_q: Why not just invest in the product with the best historical return?
questions.r3_p1: Because past performance does not predict future performance — and the data proves it.
questions.r3_p2: In the 2000s, technology funds had the best historical returns of the previous decade. Those who bet everything on them in 2000 lost 80% of their capital when the dot-com bubble burst.
questions.r3_p3: In the 2010s, emerging markets had outperformed. Investors who switched to them then suffered a decade of underperformance relative to developed markets.
questions.r3_p4: The product with the best historical return is often the one that benefited from a specific trend — and those trends reverse.
questions.r3_p5: The strategy that works over the long term is not chasing past performance, but buying the entire global economy at low cost and waiting. That is exactly what a global ETF does.
questions.r4_q: Should you wait for the right moment to invest?
questions.r4_p1: No — and the data is unambiguous on this point.
questions.r4_p2: A well-known Schwab study compared five strategies over 20 years, including two extremes: always investing at the worst moment of the year (just before each crash) vs always investing at the best moment.
questions.r4_p3: Result: even perfect timing would have generated only ~8% additional return over 20 years compared to immediate investment. By contrast, the difference between investing immediately and staying in cash waiting for the 'right moment' was 30 to 40%.
questions.r4_p4: The investor's enemy is not bad timing — it is inaction.
questions.r4_p5: The only exception: if you need this money in less than 2 years, do not invest it in equities.
questions.d1_q: How much do you need to get started?
questions.d1_p2: Most recommended brokers (MeDirect, Saxo) impose no minimum deposit. Some recent global ETFs like WEBN cost around 5 € to 10 € per share. The classics like SWRD cost ~40 €, IMIE ~220 €. Most recommended brokers allow you to start from 50 € to 100 € by buying cheaper ETFs or via automatic savings plans.
questions.d1_p3: In practice, it is advisable to invest at least 100 € to 200 € per month so that fixed transaction fees (often €0 at MeDirect) remain negligible relative to the amount invested.
questions.d1_p4: What matters more than the starting amount is regularity. 200 €/month for 30 years at 8% annual return ≈ 272 000 €. 2 400 € invested just once ≈ 24 000 € over the same period. Regularity beats the initial amount.
questions.d2_q: Is it complicated to manage on a daily basis?
questions.d2_p1: No — it is actually one of the main advantages of ETFs over other investments.
questions.d2_p2: Once your broker account is open and your first purchase made, management comes down to:
questions.d2_ul_1: A monthly transfer to your broker
questions.d2_ul_3: Doing nothing the rest of the time
questions.d2_p3: There are no daily decisions to make, no market monitoring required, no frequent rebalancing. The 'buy and hold' strategy is not only the simplest — it also statistically outperforms active trading for retail investors.
questions.d3_q: What happens if my broker goes bankrupt?
questions.d3_p1: Your ETFs are protected — this is one of the most important protections to understand.
questions.d3_p2: The ETFs you buy are not the property of the broker. They are held separately from the broker's balance sheet, in a segregated estate. If the broker goes bankrupt, your assets do not form part of the insolvent estate — they are returned to you.
questions.d3_p3: In practice, a liquidator simply transfers your positions to another broker.
questions.d3_p4: Furthermore, brokers operating in Belgium or the EU are subject to strict regulatory capital requirements. MeDirect is supervised by the Maltese MFSA and the Belgian FSMA. Saxo Bank by the Danish DFSA.
questions.d3_p5: Cash deposit protection (not ETFs) is limited to 100 000 € by the guarantee fund — an additional reason not to leave idle cash at your broker.
questions.d4_q: Should I invest everything at once or gradually?
questions.d4_p1: Both approaches work — but not for the same profiles.
questions.d4_p2: Investing all at once (lump sum) is statistically optimal: studies show that in the majority of cases (~60 to 70% depending on the study), investing the full amount immediately outperforms spreading over 12 months, because markets rise more often than they fall.
questions.d4_p3: Investing gradually (DCA — Dollar Cost Averaging) is psychologically easier: you avoid the anxiety of investing just before a fall, and you smooth your average purchase price.
questions.d4_ul_1: If you have a significant sum to invest and a long horizon (10+ years): invest in one or two instalments.
questions.d4_ul_2: If the idea of seeing -20% straight after paralyses you: spread it over 6 to 12 months. The slight statistical underperformance is well worth the peace of mind.
questions.d4_ul_3: For your regular monthly savings: invest each month automatically — that is natural DCA.
questions.d5_q: How do I place my first buy order?
questions.d5_p1: It is simpler than you imagine. In three steps:
questions.d5_ul_1: Search for the ETF by its ISIN (not by name, which varies across brokers). For example, IMIE = IE00B3YLTY66. Your broker will display the correct market line.
questions.d5_ul_2: Choose a limit order (limit order) rather than a market order. Set your limit slightly above the current price — for example 221 € if the displayed price is 220 €. This avoids unfavourable executions in case of a wide bid/ask spread.
questions.d5_ul_3: Before confirming, check the three lines of the summary: correct ISIN, TOB applied (0.12% for equity ETFs), transaction fees displayed.
questions.d5_p2: Once the order is executed, your shares appear in your portfolio within 1 to 2 business days (T+2 settlement). Nothing else to do.
questions.d5_link: → Complete guide: placing your first order step by step
questions.s1_q: Why not just buy Apple or Tesla shares?
questions.s1_p1: You can — but you are taking a structurally different risk.
questions.s1_p2: An individual share, even from an extraordinary company, can lose 70, 80, or even 100% of its value. Tesla lost 75% between 2021 and 2022. Meta lost 77% the same year. These are not obscure companies.
questions.s1_p3: The problem is not choosing the wrong company — it is that even choosing the right one, you do not know when it will outperform or underperform.
questions.s1_p4: Apple typically represents between 4% and 6% of the MSCI World index. By buying IWDA, you already own Apple — as well as Microsoft, NVIDIA, Nestlé, Toyota, and 1 400 other companies. If Apple doubles, you benefit. If Apple collapses, the impact on your portfolio is limited.
questions.s1_p5: Diversification is not a compromise on return — it is the only free protection that exists in finance.
questions.s2_q: Should you rebalance your portfolio regularly?
questions.s2_p1: For a single-ETF strategy (IMIE, all-in-one), the answer is simple: no. The ETF rebalances automatically internally. You have nothing to do.
questions.s2_p2: For a two-ETF strategy (IWDA + EMIM for example), a slight imbalance gradually develops because the two components do not grow at the same pace. An annual rebalance (selling a little of the better performer, buying the other) is more than sufficient.
questions.s2_p3: In practice: if you invest monthly, simply direct your contribution towards the ETF that is furthest below its target. This is the simplest and most tax-efficient method (no sale = no CGT).
questions.s3_q: IMIE or IWDA — which to choose?
questions.s3_p1: Both are excellent choices. The difference is smaller than you think.
questions.s3_p2: IWDA (MSCI World): ~1 400 companies, developed markets only, ~70% US. TER 0.20%. Slightly better over the past 20 years because emerging markets underperformed.
questions.s3_p3: IMIE (MSCI ACWI IMI): ~9 000 companies, developed + emerging markets + small caps. TER 0.17%. Maximum diversification, slightly more representative of the global economy.
questions.s3_p4: Our recommendation: IMIE for a beginner who wants a definitive solution without asking further questions. IWDA if you are comfortable with a more concentrated exposure to developed markets.
questions.s3_p5: Both have the same Belgian tax treatment (TOB 0.12%, no Reynders tax, same CGT). The performance difference over 30 years will probably be less than 0.5% per year.
questions.f1_q: Do I have to declare my ETFs to the tax authorities?
questions.f1_p2: With a Belgian broker (MeDirect, Saxo Bank Belgium, Bolero, Keytrade): the TOB is withheld and declared automatically at each transaction. You have nothing to do for the TOB.
questions.f1_p3: Capital gains (CGT 10% above €10,000/year) must be declared in your annual tax return. Your broker provides you with an annual transaction statement that simplifies this declaration.
questions.f1_p4: With certain foreign brokers (Interactive Brokers, Trade Republic): you are responsible for calculating and declaring the TOB yourself every two months — a real administrative burden with risk of fines in case of delay.
questions.f1_p5: Other popular foreign brokers like DEGIRO now automatically handle the TOB for their Belgian clients, but the précompte mobilier and CGT 2026 still need to be declared manually. Always check your broker's current terms — these practices may evolve.
questions.f2_q: What happens tax-wise when I sell?
questions.f2_p1: Three taxes may apply on sale, depending on your situation:
questions.f2_ul_1: TOB (0.12%): withheld automatically by your broker on the total sale amount. Always due, regardless of the capital gain.
questions.f2_ul_2: CGT (10%): due only on the realised capital gain, above the annual exemption of €10,000. Example: capital gain of €8,000 — below the exemption, no CGT. Capital gain of €15,000 — you pay 10% on €5,000 = €500.
questions.f2_ul_3: Reynders tax (30% on the bond portion): does not apply to purely equity ETFs like IMIE, IWDA, CSPX. Only for mixed funds containing bonds.
questions.f2_p2: For the vast majority of investors with a standard equity ETF portfolio, only the TOB and possibly the CGT apply.
questions.s4_q: Why not real estate instead of ETFs?
questions.s4_p1: Real estate is not a bad strategy — it's a different strategy, which requires much more than just money.
questions.s4_p2: **What is leverage?** It's the act of using the bank's money to increase your investment capacity. A bank will lend you for bricks, not for ETFs.
questions.s4_p3: Without leverage, Belgian real estate has historically delivered 6% to 8% per year — comparable to a global ETF. With leverage (e.g., €50k down payment for a €250k property), your return on initial investment could reach 15% to 20% during the era of low rates.
questions.s4_p4: But this leverage is only beneficial if the property's yield is higher than the mortgage rate. With the rise in rates, this effect can become neutral or even destroy value if the cost of borrowing exceeds the net yield.
questions.s4_p5: Add to this 15% entry costs, total illiquidity, and active management that feels like a second job. ETFs win on simplicity, liquidity, and time.
questions.s4_link: → Full analysis: Real Estate vs ETF
questions.s4_p6: Both can coexist in a balanced wealth strategy.

premierAchat.hero_subtitle: Step by step, from finding the ETF to confirming the order — without unnecessary jargon.
premierAchat.s1_intro: Three conditions must be met before placing your first order:
premierAchat.s1_item1_desc: You know which ETF you want to buy and have its ISIN to hand.
premierAchat.s1_item2_desc: Your account is open, verified (KYC), and you can log in.
premierAchat.s1_item3_desc: A transfer has been made to your broker account and the funds are available (generally 1 to 2 business days after the transfer).
premierAchat.s2_intro: Never search for an ETF by name — the same ETF can be named differently depending on the broker and stock exchange. The ISIN is the universal identifier: it never changes, regardless of the exchange.
premierAchat.s2_imie_name: iShares MSCI ACWI IMI — entire world
premierAchat.s2_iwda_name: iShares Core MSCI World — developed markets
premierAchat.s2_emim_name: iShares Core MSCI EM IMI — emerging markets
premierAchat.s2_xmaw_name: Xtrackers MSCI All World — entire world (alternative)
premierAchat.s2_xeon_name: Xtrackers II EUR Overnight Rate Swap — money market
premierAchat.s2_tip: Paste the ISIN directly into your broker's search bar. Several results may appear (different exchanges: Euronext Amsterdam, London, Xetra). For a Belgian resident, prefer the EUR listing on Euronext Amsterdam to avoid currency risk.
premierAchat.s3_intro: You will be offered two types of orders:
premierAchat.s3_market_desc: Executed immediately at the best available price. Simple, but you have no control over the final price — especially risky outside peak liquidity hours.
premierAchat.s3_limit_desc: You set the maximum price you are willing to pay. The order is only executed at that price or better. You retain control.
premierAchat.s3_tip: For a limit order, set your limit 0.5 to 1% above the currently displayed price. Example: displayed price €220.00 → limit at €221.00 or €222.00. The margin covers the bid/ask spread and guarantees execution under normal market conditions.
premierAchat.s4_intro: Before clicking 'Confirm', systematically check these three points on the summary screen:
premierAchat.s4_item1_desc: Compare character by character with your reference. One different character means a different product.
premierAchat.s4_item2_desc: For an equity ETF, the TOB should be 0.12% of the transaction amount. If you see 1.32%, the ETF may be registered as a UCITS — check the ISIN.
premierAchat.s4_item3_desc: Your broker must clearly state the fees before confirmation. At MeDirect, ETFs have €0 transaction fees.
premierAchat.s4_warning: If the displayed TOB is 1.32% instead of 0.12%, stop and do not confirm. This indicates that the selected ETF is registered as a UCITS in Belgium — most often a selection error (wrong market line). Go back and check the ISIN.
premierAchat.s5_p1: Once the order is executed, your shares appear in your portfolio after settlement — generally within 2 business days (T+2). This is normal: it is the standard settlement cycle in Europe.
premierAchat.s5_p2: You will receive a transaction confirmation by email or in your broker platform. Keep these confirmations — they will be useful when declaring your capital gain (CGT) at the appropriate time.
premierAchat.s5_p3: Next step: decide on a regular investment frequency. Monthly is the simplest rhythm for most investors.
premierAchat.s5_tip: Several brokers (MeDirect, Saxo) offer automatic transfers or periodic investment plans. Setting up a monthly automatic transfer to your broker account removes the monthly decision and eliminates the temptation to wait for the 'right moment'.
premierAchat.s6_p1: The recommended ETFs are listed primarily on Euronext Amsterdam, open Monday to Friday from 9:00 to 17:30 CET. Outside these hours, your order is queued and executed at the next opening.
premierAchat.s6_p2: For a long-term investor, the exact time of the order has virtually no impact. That said, avoid the first and last 15 minutes of the session (9:00–9:15 and 17:15–17:30): bid/ask spreads are slightly wider at opening and close. The 10:00–16:00 window generally offers the best liquidity.
premierAchat.disclaimer: This guide is provided for educational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice. The ISINs, fees, and broker terms mentioned are given as an indication and may change.

declarerCompte.hero_title: Declaring an account with a foreign broker
declarerCompte.hero_subtitle: Two simple obligations — not to be forgotten under penalty of fines.
declarerCompte.intro_p1: If you invest via a Belgian broker (MeDirect, Saxo, Bolero, Keytrade, Re=Bel), you have nothing to declare beyond your normal annual tax return. This guide does not apply to you.
declarerCompte.intro_p2: If you use a foreign broker (DEGIRO, Interactive Brokers, Trade Republic, or other), Belgian law imposes two separate administrative obligations on you. They are simple but mandatory — ignoring them exposes you to fines.
declarerCompte.intro_info: This guide applies to any broker whose registered office is outside Belgium, regardless of its popularity or reputation. The nationality of the broker, not that of the investor, determines the obligation.
declarerCompte.s1_title: 1. Declaring to the Central Contact Point of the NBB
declarerCompte.s1_p1: The National Bank of Belgium (NBB) maintains a central register of all foreign accounts held by Belgian residents — the Central Contact Point (PCC).
declarerCompte.s1_p2: You must declare your account there once, as soon as you open it. This declaration is definitive and does not need to be renewed each year.
declarerCompte.s1_step1_desc: Log in via itsme or eID card reader — same interface as Tax-on-web.
declarerCompte.s1_step2_desc: The site is available in French, Dutch, and English.
declarerCompte.s1_step3_desc: Or 'Uw buitenlandse bankrekeningen aangeven' in Dutch.
declarerCompte.s1_step4_intro: Click on 'Add an account' and fill in the following fields:
declarerCompte.s1_step4_note: Note: some brokers separate the securities account (holding your ETFs) from the cash account (holding your cash). If so, declare both separately.
declarerCompte.s1_field1_desc: Your username or account number at the broker
declarerCompte.s1_field2_desc: Full legal name of the broker
declarerCompte.s1_field3_desc: Country of the broker's registered office
declarerCompte.s1_field5_desc: Only if the account is closed
declarerCompte.s1_field7_desc: Address of the broker's registered office
declarerCompte.s1_step5_desc: Keep a copy of the summary for your records.
declarerCompte.s1_tip: When to declare? Ideally within the weeks following the account opening, and in any case before submitting your tax return for the year in question. Example: account opened in October 2025 → declare before your May/June 2026 tax return.
declarerCompte.s2_title: 2. Mentioning the account in your annual tax return
declarerCompte.s2_p1: Each year, as long as you hold an account with a foreign broker, you must mention it in your annual tax return (Tax-on-web).
declarerCompte.s2_p2: This is not an additional income declaration — it is simply a box to tick confirming the existence of the account.
declarerCompte.s2_item1_desc: Tick 'Yes' to indicate that you hold one or more foreign accounts.
declarerCompte.s2_item2_desc: Add one row per account with your name, the broker's country, and confirm that the NBB declaration has been made.
declarerCompte.s2_tip: If you have several foreign accounts (e.g. DEGIRO + Interactive Brokers), add a separate row for each.
declarerCompte.s3_intro: Beyond the administrative declarations, some foreign brokers do not automatically handle all Belgian taxes. Here is what you need to check for your broker:
declarerCompte.s3_manual_intro: For taxes to be declared manually:
declarerCompte.s3_tob_desc: The TOB must be declared and paid by the end of the 2nd month following each transaction, via the form available on the FPS Finance website. Payment by bank transfer with the structured reference provided in the form.
declarerCompte.s3_precompte_desc: If you hold distributing ETFs or individual shares, the 30% précompte on dividends must be declared annually in your tax return if your foreign broker does not withhold it. By using accumulating ETFs (Acc.), you avoid this obligation — dividends are automatically reinvested without distribution.
declarerCompte.s3_cgt_desc: Capital gains realised on the sale of ETFs must be declared in your annual tax return. Your broker must provide you with an annual transaction statement to facilitate this calculation.
declarerCompte.s3_tip: The simplest solution to avoid all this administrative complexity: use a Belgian broker (MeDirect, Saxo) that handles all taxes automatically.
declarerCompte.s4_p1: Belgium, like all EU countries, participates in the automatic exchange of tax information between member states (DAC/CRS directive). Your foreign broker shares your data with the tax authorities of its country, which forwards it to the Belgian FPS Finance.
declarerCompte.s4_p2: In practice: the Belgian tax authorities already know you have this account. The declaration is a compliance formality, not a revelation.
declarerCompte.s4_warning_title: Failing to declare exposes you to:
declarerCompte.s4_bullet1: A fine of €100 to €1,000 per undeclared account
declarerCompte.s4_bullet2: A risk of increased taxation on undeclared income
declarerCompte.disclaimer: This information is provided for educational purposes and reflects the obligations in force in 2026. Administrative procedures may change — always check the current instructions at cappcc.nbb.be and minfin.fgov.be.

glossaire.hero_subtitle: The essential investment terms, explained simply.
glossaire.term_etf_def: An ETF is an investment fund listed on the stock exchange that automatically replicates the performance of a stock index — for example the 1,400 largest companies in the world. Unlike an actively managed fund, nobody chooses the shares: the fund mechanically follows the index, which drastically reduces costs.
glossaire.term_indice_def: An index is a list of companies selected according to defined criteria (size, country, sector). The MSCI World groups the ~1,400 largest companies in developed countries. The MSCI ACWI IMI covers ~9,000 including emerging markets and small caps. Investing in an ETF that tracks an index means investing in all those companies at once.
glossaire.term_diversification_def: Diversification means spreading your capital across many different assets to reduce risk. If one company goes bankrupt in a portfolio of 9,000 companies, the impact is negligible. A global ETF provides instant, maximum diversification in a single purchase.
glossaire.term_volatilite_def: Volatility measures the magnitude of price fluctuations of an asset over time. A global equity ETF is volatile in the short term — it can lose 30% in a few months during a crisis. But over the long term, this volatility is offset by the structural upward trend of markets. Volatility is not the same as a permanent loss.
glossaire.term_dividende_def: A dividend is a portion of profits that a company pays to its shareholders. Some ETFs distribute these dividends as cash (distributing ETFs), others automatically reinvest them in the fund (accumulating ETFs). In Belgium, dividends received are taxed at 30% — which is why accumulating ETFs are generally preferable.
glossaire.term_plus_value_def: A capital gain is the profit realised on the sale of an asset at a higher price than its purchase price. Since 1 January 2026, capital gains on financial assets are taxed at 10% in Belgium, above an annual exemption of €10,000.
glossaire.term_moins_value_def: A capital loss is a loss incurred on the sale of an asset at a lower price than its purchase price. Capital losses can be offset against capital gains of the same year, but cannot be carried forward to subsequent years.
glossaire.term_capitalisation_def: The market capitalisation of a company is the total value of its shares in circulation (share price × number of shares). Stock market indices are generally weighted by capitalisation: the more a company is worth, the greater its share in the index.
glossaire.term_accumulant_def: An accumulating ETF automatically reinvests dividends within the fund, without distributing them as cash. For a Belgian investor, this is tax-advantageous: reinvested dividends do not trigger the 30% précompte mobilier. The ETFs recommended on ClearInvest are almost exclusively accumulating.
glossaire.term_distribuant_def: An distributing ETF regularly pays dividends as cash to your broker account. In Belgium, these dividends are subject to 30% précompte mobilier, which reduces the compounding effect over the long term. Distributing ETFs may suit investors who want a regular income.
glossaire.term_ter_def: The TER is the annual management cost of an ETF, expressed as a percentage. A TER of 0.17% means that on €10,000 invested, €17 is taken each year by the fund manager. This cost is already reflected in the ETF's performance — it is not a separate invoice. Index ETFs have TERs between 0.07% and 0.30%, compared to 1.20% to 2.50% for actively managed bank funds.
glossaire.term_isin_def: The ISIN (International Securities Identification Number) is the unique identification code of an ETF or share. It consists of 12 characters (e.g. IE00B3YLTY66 for IMIE). Always search for an ETF by its ISIN at your broker to avoid confusion with funds of similar names.
glossaire.term_dca_def: DCA means investing a fixed amount at regular intervals (e.g. €200 each month), regardless of market levels. This strategy automatically smooths the average purchase price: you buy more shares when prices fall, fewer when they rise. This is the strategy adopted by most retail investors who invest their monthly savings.
glossaire.term_lump_sum_def: Lump sum investing means investing a large amount all at once rather than spreading it over time. Studies show that in approximately 60 to 70% of cases, investing immediately outperforms DCA over the long term, because markets rise more often than they fall. Psychologically, DCA often remains more comfortable.
glossaire.term_reequilibrage_def: Rebalancing means adjusting the composition of your portfolio to return to the target allocation. For a single-ETF portfolio (e.g. IMIE), rebalancing is automatic — the ETF updates internally. For a multi-ETF portfolio, a light annual rebalance is generally sufficient, by directing new contributions towards the underweight ETF.
glossaire.term_fonds_actif_def: An active fund is managed by professionals who select which shares to buy and sell. Costs are high (1.20% to 2.50% per year) to remunerate this management. Between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark index over 20 years after fees — making it difficult to justify their additional cost compared to an index ETF.
glossaire.term_fonds_indiciel_def: An index fund mechanically replicates a stock market index without human intervention. ETFs are the most accessible form of index fund. Their very low costs (often below 0.20% per year) constitute their main structural advantage over the long term.
glossaire.term_tob_name: TOB (taks op beursverrichtingen / stock exchange tax)
glossaire.term_tob_def: The TOB is a Belgian tax levied on each purchase and sale of ETFs or shares. For almost all ETFs recommended on ClearInvest (IMIE, IWDA, CSPX…), the rate is 0.12% — i.e. €12 on €10,000 invested. For ETFs registered in Belgium (such as VWCE), the rate rises to 1.32%. With a Belgian broker, the TOB is withheld and declared automatically.
glossaire.term_precompte_name: Précompte mobilier (withholding tax on movable income)
glossaire.term_precompte_def: The précompte mobilier is a withholding tax of 30% on income from movable assets (dividends, interest). For regulated savings accounts, a preferential rate of 15% applies above the annual exemption of €1,020. Accumulating ETFs avoid the précompte on dividends because these are automatically reinvested without being distributed.
glossaire.term_reynders_def: The Reynders tax is a 30% tax on the bond component of the capital gain on the sale of a capitalisation fund containing more than 10% bonds. It does not apply to purely equity ETFs (IMIE, IWDA, CSPX, etc.). It concerns mixed funds and certain bond ETFs.
glossaire.term_cgt_def: Since 1 January 2026, capital gains on financial assets are taxed at 10% in Belgium, above an annual exemption of €10,000. The unused exemption can be carried forward: up to €1,000 per year for a maximum of 5 years, giving a cumulative exemption of up to €15,000. This tax applies on the sale of your ETFs.
glossaire.term_broker_def: A broker is a platform that allows you to buy and sell ETFs and shares on the stock market. In Belgium, the recommended brokers for passive ETF investing are MeDirect (0% fees on ETFs) and Saxo Bank (AutoInvest at €2/month). Belgian brokers automatically handle the TOB declaration.
glossaire.term_branche21_def: Branch 21 is a savings insurance product with a guaranteed interest rate, subject to a 2% premium tax on each contribution. Interest is exempt from précompte mobilier after 8 years of holding. Structurally disadvantageous compared to an ETF over the long term due to entry fees, the premium tax, and the limited guaranteed return.
glossaire.term_branche23_def: Branch 23 is a life insurance product linked to investment funds, with no guaranteed return. Used notably for pension savings (épargne-pension) via bank funds. The anticipatory tax of 8% at age 60 is calculated on a notional capital assuming an annual return of 4.75%.
glossaire.term_epargne_pension_def: Belgian pension savings (3rd pillar) allow a 30% tax reduction on contributions up to ~€1,050/year. The capital is locked until age 60 and subject to an exit tax of 8%. Despite the initial tax advantage, an ETF structurally outperforms pension savings from year 11 onwards due to the return differential.

monde-simplifie.name: Simplified World
monde-simplifie.description: A single ETF that replicates the performance of the largest global companies. The simplest strategy for exposure to developed markets — no rebalancing necessary.
monde-simplifie.historicalReturnNote: Based on the MSCI World index in EUR, dividends reinvested
monde-simplifie.why_0: 0.12% TOB: the accumulating share class of IWDA is not registered in Belgium with the FSMA — the reduced 0.12% stock exchange tax applies to purchase and sale.
monde-simplifie.why_1: No annual withholding tax on dividends: IWDA is an accumulating fund — dividends are automatically reinvested in the fund, without deduction of the 30% Belgian withholding tax (précompte mobilier).
monde-simplifie.why_2: Diversification in 1 click: the MSCI World index covers approximately 1,400 of the largest companies across 23 developed countries — United States, Europe, Japan, etc. — in their natural proportions.
monde-simplifie.why_3: Competitive TER: at 0.20%/year, it is one of the most accessible world ETFs on the Belgian market.
monde-simplifie.alt_0_verdict: Excellent choice if your broker offers free transactions on SPDR. The lower TER compensates for the slightly lower liquidity over the long term.
monde-simplifie.alt_1_pro_0: Includes emerging markets + small caps (~9,000 companies according to the MSCI ACWI IMI index, ~4,500 held via representative sampling)
monde-simplifie.alt_1_con_1: Slightly more volatile (exposure to emerging markets)
monde-simplifie.alt_1_verdict: The natural upgrade if you want complete global diversification. This is the "All-in-one" strategy of this site.
monde-simplifie.alt_2_con_0: TOB potentially 1.32% depending on the broker — some apply 0.12%, others 1.32%. Check before investing
monde-simplifie.alt_2_verdict: To be avoided for Belgian investors — the applicable TOB varies by broker — from 0.12% to 1.32%. In the worst case, you pay €132 in taxes on €10,000 compared to €12 with IMIE. Essential to check with your broker before any purchase.

monde-complet.name: Complete World
monde-complet.description: Combines developed (89%) and emerging (11%) markets for truly global exposure, including China, India, and Brazil. Slightly more volatile but more representative of the global economy.
monde-complet.historicalReturnNote: Slightly higher than IMIE thanks to the absence of small caps, which underperformed large/mid caps over the 2005-2025 period. The emerging component (11%) has little weight on the overall return.
monde-complet.why_0: 0.12% TOB on both ETFs: IWDA (developed markets) and EMIM (emerging markets) are both subject to the reduced 0.12% TOB, unlike some all-in-one world ETFs.
monde-complet.why_1: Exposure to emerging markets: by adding EMIM (11%), you invest in high-growth economies — China, India, Brazil, Taiwan, Korea — underrepresented in a pure world ETF.
monde-complet.why_2: 100% accumulating funds: no annual withholding tax on dividends (accumulating funds — dividends automatically reinvested in both funds).
monde-complet.why_3: Allocation control: you can adjust the emerging/developed weighting according to your convictions (e.g. 80/20 rather than 89/11) — impossible with an all-in-one ETF.
monde-complet.alt_0_verdict: If you want the same exposure but in 1 single ETF, IMIE is simpler and not more expensive.
monde-complet.alt_1_pro_0: 0.15% TER — cheaper than IWDA + EMIM combined
monde-complet.alt_1_con_1: Tracks the FTSE index (not MSCI) — slight composition differences
monde-complet.alt_1_verdict: Good alternative if you are looking for an all-in-one cheaper than IMIE without small caps.
monde-complet.alt_2_con_0: TOB potentially 1.32% depending on the broker — some apply 0.12%, others 1.32%. Check before investing
monde-complet.alt_2_verdict: To be avoided for Belgian investors — the applicable TOB varies by broker — from 0.12% to 1.32%. In the worst case, you pay €132 in taxes on €10,000 compared to €12 with IMIE. Essential to check with your broker before any purchase.

tout-en-un.name: All-in-one
tout-en-un.tagline: The whole world in a single ETF
tout-en-un.description: A unique ETF that covers developed and emerging markets in their natural proportions. Ideal for those who want absolute simplicity: a single purchase, zero rebalancing.
tout-en-un.historicalReturnNote: Slightly lower than Complete World because IMIE includes small caps (global large, mid AND small cap), which marginally underperformed over the 2005-2025 period. In return, IMIE offers more complete diversification (~99% of global market cap).
tout-en-un.why_0: 0.12% TOB: unlike VWCE which is registered in Belgium and subject to 1.32% TOB, IMIE is not registered in Belgium — you only pay 0.12% on purchase and sale.
tout-en-un.why_1: No annual withholding tax on dividends: IMIE is an accumulating fund — dividends are automatically reinvested, without triggering the 30% withholding tax.
tout-en-un.why_2: No Reynders tax (purely equity ETF, 0% bonds): this tax applies only to funds holding more than 10% bonds — IMIE is exempt.
tout-en-un.why_3: Maximum diversification: the MSCI ACWI IMI index covers ~9,000 companies according to the index (the ETF holds ~4,500 via representative sampling — the effective diversification is equivalent), covering developed, emerging, and small cap markets — about 99% of the investable global stock market.
tout-en-un.alt_0_pro_0: Extremely low TER (0.07%) — the cheapest on the market
tout-en-un.alt_0_con_0: Launched in June 2024 — very short history
tout-en-un.alt_0_con_1: Tracks the Solactive index (lesser known than MSCI)
tout-en-un.alt_0_con_3: Amundi has a history of mergers and index rule changes
tout-en-un.alt_0_verdict: Interesting for its cost, but lacks track record and excludes small caps.
tout-en-un.alt_1_pro_0: IWDA is Europe's most liquid ETF
tout-en-un.alt_1_con_1: Two transactions = two sets of brokerage fees at each purchase
tout-en-un.alt_1_verdict: Good alternative if your broker favours IWDA, but IMIE does all this automatically.
tout-en-un.alt_2_con_1: No small caps (large & mid cap only)
tout-en-un.alt_2_verdict: Good budget option if small caps don't matter to you.
tout-en-un.alt_3_con_0: TOB potentially 1.32% depending on the broker — some apply 0.12%, others 1.32%. Check before investing
tout-en-un.alt_3_verdict: To be avoided for Belgian investors — the applicable TOB varies by broker — from 0.12% to 1.32%. In the worst case, you pay €132 in taxes on €10,000 compared to €12 with IMIE. Essential to check with your broker before any purchase.

esg-mondiale.name: Global ESG
esg-mondiale.description: Global exposure to developed markets with an ESG filter that excludes controversial companies (weapons, tobacco, thermal coal). Historical performance close to the unfiltered global market.
esg-mondiale.historicalReturnNote: Slightly outperformed IWDA since 2012 (+0.4%/year) thanks to the exclusion of sectors that underperformed (fossil fuels, tobacco). Data prior to 2012 is based on a backtest, not real data. This outperformance is not guaranteed in the future.
esg-mondiale.why_0: ESG Screened exclusions: XMAW excludes companies involved in controversial weapons, tobacco, thermal coal, and companies with serious ESG standard violations — about 10 to 15% of MSCI World companies are filtered out.
esg-mondiale.why_1: Market-like performance: the MSCI World ESG Screened index has historically tracked the classic MSCI World very closely — exclusions have not significantly penalised the 20-year return.
esg-mondiale.why_2: 0.12% TOB and accumulating fund: same tax advantages as IWDA — no withholding tax on dividends, reduced TOB.
esg-mondiale.why_3: "Screened" vs "SRI" approach: XMAW applies a light exclusion filter (major controversies) rather than a strict positive ESG score — which maintains broad diversification while excluding the most problematic cases.
esg-mondiale.alt_0_pro_0: Stricter SRI criteria (positive ESG score required)
esg-mondiale.alt_0_con_2: Performance may deviate more from the market
esg-mondiale.alt_0_verdict: For investors desiring more demanding ESG criteria, at the cost of less diversification.
esg-mondiale.alt_1_pro_0: Very low TER (0.07%) — the cheapest among global ESG ETFs
esg-mondiale.alt_1_pro_1: Same ESG Screened exclusions as XMAW
esg-mondiale.alt_1_verdict: Excellent for reducing costs while keeping a similar ESG filter — if you are comfortable with synthetic replication.
esg-mondiale.alt_2_con_0: No ESG filter — includes all industries
esg-mondiale.alt_2_con_1: Same TER as XMAW for broader exposure
esg-mondiale.alt_2_verdict: If ESG criteria are secondary for you, IWDA offers maximum liquidity at the same cost.

biais-europe.name: Europe Bias
biais-europe.description: Combines a global base (60%) with an overweighting of Europe (40%). Reduces exposure to the United States compared to a pure World ETF, with catch-up potential if European markets outperform.
biais-europe.historicalReturnNote: Europe slightly underperformed the US over this period but offers better current valuation
biais-europe.why_0: Reduction of US concentration: a classic world ETF allocates ~70% to the United States. This strategy brings that exposure down to ~45% via the overweighting of Europe (40%), providing more geographical balance.
biais-europe.why_1: 0.12% TOB on both ETFs: IWDA and IEUR are both subject to the reduced 0.12% TOB in Belgium.
biais-europe.why_2: Attractive European valuation: historically, European markets trade at lower valuation levels than US markets — which may represent catch-up potential.
biais-europe.why_3: 100% accumulating funds: dividends are automatically reinvested in both ETFs, without withholding tax.
biais-europe.alt_0_pro_0: Concentration on the 50 largest European companies
biais-europe.alt_0_pro_1: Lower TER on the European part (0.10%)
biais-europe.alt_0_con_0: Only 50 companies = less diversified than IEUR
biais-europe.alt_0_con_3: Eurozone coverage only — excludes UK, Switzerland, Sweden (unlike IEUR which covers all of developed Europe)
biais-europe.alt_0_verdict: For investors preferring European blue chips over mid caps.
biais-europe.alt_1_pro_1: Full global diversification including small caps
biais-europe.alt_1_con_0: Fixed Europe weighting (dictated by the index)
biais-europe.alt_1_verdict: If you do not want to manage two ETFs, IMIE offers global exposure without active bias.
biais-europe.alt_2_pro_1: Good coverage of developed European markets
biais-europe.alt_2_con_0: Distributing fund — dividends subject to 30% withholding tax
biais-europe.alt_2_con_1: Can only be coupled with IWDA — requires rebalancing
biais-europe.alt_2_con_2: Slight composition difference vs IEUR (FTSE vs MSCI)
biais-europe.alt_2_verdict: Lower TER than IEUR, but the distributing nature generates tax friction (30% withholding on dividends). For an investor in the accumulation phase, IEUR is generally preferable.

anti-us.name: Anti-US
anti-us.tagline: Reducing exposure to the United States
anti-us.description: For investors who want to significantly reduce their dependence on the United States (which represents ~65% of a World ETF). Diversifies towards Europe and China.
anti-us.historicalReturnNote: China weighed on returns over this period (~4%/year). This strategy is forward-looking and not optimised for past performance
anti-us.why_0: Drastic reduction of US exposure: in a classic world ETF, the United States represents ~70%. Here, via IWDA (60%) + overweighting Europe (20%) + China (20%), the effective US share drops to about 42%.
anti-us.why_1: 0.12% TOB on the three ETFs: IWDA, IEUR, and ICHN are all subject to the reduced 0.12% TOB in Belgium.
anti-us.why_2: Multi-regional diversification: this strategy bets on the convergence between developed economies (Europe, Japan via IWDA) and catch-up economies (China) to reduce dependence on a single market.
anti-us.why_3: 100% accumulating funds: all dividends are automatically reinvested, without withholding tax.
anti-us.alt_0_verdict: If you want to reduce US exposure without specifically betting on China alone, IWDA + EMIM is simpler.
anti-us.alt_1_pro_1: Includes small caps and emerging markets in natural proportions
anti-us.alt_1_con_0: US at ~65% — less reduction than this strategy
anti-us.alt_1_verdict: For those who simply want to avoid pure VWCE/IWDA without a pronounced active bias.

biais-chine.name: China Bias
biais-chine.description: Adds significant exposure to China (30%) beyond what a standard World ETF includes (~3%). Suitable for investors convinced of the long-term catch-up potential of Chinese markets.
biais-chine.historicalReturnNote: China underperformed over the past 20 years. This strategy carries significant country risk
biais-chine.why_0: Deliberate overweighting of China: a classic world ETF allocates only ~3% to China. This strategy allocates 30% — 10× more — for investors convinced of the Chinese market catch-up.
biais-chine.why_1: 0.12% TOB on both ETFs: IWDA and ICHN are both subject to the reduced 0.12% TOB in Belgium.
biais-chine.why_2: 100% accumulating funds: dividends automatically reinvested without withholding tax.
biais-chine.why_3: Exposure to the Chinese equity market via ICHN: the iShares MSCI China ETF covers H-shares (listed in Hong Kong) and accessible A-shares, giving direct exposure to listed Chinese companies.
biais-chine.alt_0_pro_0: Diversified emerging exposure (India, Brazil, Taiwan + China)
biais-chine.alt_0_pro_1: Less concentration on a single country
biais-chine.alt_0_con_0: China at only ~6% — much less than 30% here
biais-chine.alt_0_con_1: No fine control of the China weighting
biais-chine.alt_0_verdict: If you want emerging exposure without betting as much on China alone.
biais-chine.alt_1_pro_1: For investors with a strong China conviction
biais-chine.alt_1_con_1: High volatility, strong sensitivity to government regulation
biais-chine.alt_1_con_2: Marked underperformance over the past 5 years
biais-chine.alt_1_verdict: Only if you want 100% China exposure. Very high risk — to be reserved for a small fraction of the portfolio.

biais-us.name: US Bias
biais-us.description: Concentrated exposure to the 500 largest US companies via the S&P 500. One of the best performing indices historically, with a very low TER. High geographical concentration.
biais-us.historicalReturnNote: Includes a favourable USD/EUR currency effect over this period. Past performance is not indicative of future results
biais-us.why_0: TER among the lowest on the market: at 0.07%/year, CSPX (iShares Core S&P 500) is one of the cheapest ETFs available in Europe — that is €7 annual fees per €10,000 invested.
biais-us.why_1: 0.12% TOB and accumulating fund: dividends automatically reinvested, reduced TOB on purchase and sale.
biais-us.why_2: The S&P 500: 500 of the largest companies listed in the US, representing about 80% of total US market capitalisation. The index delivered ~11.5%/year between 2005 and 2025.
biais-us.why_3: Deliberate concentration: this strategy is an explicit bet on the dominance of US companies — technology, finance, healthcare. It carries a USD/EUR currency risk and geographical concentration risk.
biais-us.alt_0_pro_1: Reputable Vanguard brand known for stability
biais-us.alt_0_verdict: Equivalent alternative to CSPX — the choice between the two depends on your broker and transaction fees.
biais-us.alt_1_pro_0: Same fund as CSPX, denominated in EUR
biais-us.alt_1_con_0: EUR listing — value still reflects USD/EUR fluctuations
biais-us.alt_1_con_1: Slightly less liquid than CSPX on some brokers
biais-us.alt_1_verdict: Identical to CSPX in practice — choose based on your broker's preferred listing (USD on LSE vs EUR on Xetra).
biais-us.alt_2_con_1: Historical return lower than S&P 500 over 20 years (but more stable)
biais-us.alt_2_verdict: If you want to reduce US geographical concentration risk while maintaining global exposure to developed markets.

tech-us.name: US Tech / Nasdaq
tech-us.description: Exposure to the 100 largest tech companies on the Nasdaq. High historical returns but higher volatility than a global ETF.
tech-us.historicalReturnNote: Exceptional performance driven by US tech giants. High volatility (-33% in 2022). Past performance is not indicative of future results
tech-us.why_0: The Nasdaq-100 in detail: CNDX replicates the 100 largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq — dominated by Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, Alphabet. These top 10 values represent ~50% of the index.
tech-us.why_1: 0.12% TOB and accumulating fund: dividends automatically reinvested, reduced TOB on purchase and sale.
tech-us.why_2: Exceptional but volatile historical performance: ~13%/year between 2005 and 2025, but also -33% in 2022 alone. This strategy is for investors with a long horizon and high volatility tolerance.
tech-us.why_3: Sector concentration: about 65% of the fund is in the technology sector — very strong exposure to a single sector and a single country (United States).
tech-us.alt_0_pro_1: One of the oldest Nasdaq ETFs in Europe (launched in 2002)
tech-us.alt_0_con_0: Distributing ETF — 30% withholding tax on paid dividends
tech-us.alt_0_verdict: Same index, but distributing = 30% withholding tax on dividends. Prefer CNDX for Belgian tax efficiency.
tech-us.alt_1_con_0: Lower liquidity than CNDX and EQQQ
tech-us.alt_1_verdict: Good accumulating alternative with intermediate TER if CNDX is not available at your broker.
tech-us.alt_2_con_0: Less tech concentration — lower potential returns
tech-us.alt_2_verdict: If you want US exposure but with less tech sector concentration — an S&P 500 diversifies more for the same total cost.
tech-us.warning_0: ⚠️ High sector concentration: this fund invests mainly in US tech. Volatility is significantly higher than a global ETF. Exceptional past returns (~13%/year) do not guarantee future performance.

dividendes.name: Income & Dividends
dividendes.description: Selection of global companies with high dividends. Distributes dividends regularly, providing an income stream. Tax-inefficient compared to an accumulating ETF in Belgium.
dividendes.historicalReturnNote: Total return before 30% tax on dividends. After Belgian taxation, the effective return is significantly lower
dividendes.why_0: Regular passive income: VHYL pays dividends quarterly (~4%/year dividend yield) — useful for investors needing regular liquidity without selling their shares.
dividendes.why_1: Income-oriented global diversification: the ETF selects global companies with high dividends — utilities, financials, energy — in ~50 developed and emerging countries.
dividendes.why_2: 0.12% TOB: VHYL benefits from the reduced 0.12% TOB, unlike some equivalent ETFs.
dividendes.why_3: Important tax warning: in Belgium, each received dividend is subject to 30% withholding tax. On a 4% dividend yield, this represents 1.2% extra annual tax costs compared to an equivalent accumulating ETF — a significant impact over 20 years.
dividendes.alt_0_pro_1: Historically higher total return (growth + reinvested dividends)
dividendes.alt_0_con_0: No regular income — shares must be sold to obtain liquidity
dividendes.alt_0_verdict: For almost all Belgian investors in the accumulation phase, IWDA is more tax-efficient than VHYL over the long term.
dividendes.alt_1_verdict: If you like VHYL's dividend selection but want to avoid the withholding tax, VHYA is the official accumulating version — more tax-appropriate for Belgian investors.
dividendes.warning_0: ⚠️ Distributing ETF: dividends received are subject to a 30% tax in Belgium. This strategy suits investors wanting regular income, but it is less tax-efficient than an accumulating ETF.

epargne-liquide.name: Liquid Savings
epargne-liquide.description: A smart alternative to the Belgian savings account. XEON tracks the European interbank rate (€STR) and offers returns close to the ECB policy rate with near-zero volatility and full liquidity.
epargne-liquide.historicalReturnNote: Tracks the €STR rate (formerly EONIA). Yield varies according to the ECB policy rate
epargne-liquide.why_0: Current yield ~3% gross, higher than the best Belgian savings accounts (base rate + loyalty bonus)
epargne-liquide.why_1: Fully liquid — sellable any business day without loss of accrued interest, unlike bons de caisse
epargne-liquide.why_2: Synthetic structure: XEON physically holds shares and receives the €STR rate via a swap — currently tax-advantageous: the Reynders tax does not in principle apply (synthetic structure), but this treatment is subject to evolving tax interpretation
epargne-liquide.why_3: Main tax: 0.12% TOB on purchase and sale — no annual withholding tax, no capital gains tax (below the €10,000 threshold). Reynders: in principle not applicable, but tax treatment to be monitored.
epargne-liquide.why_4: Near-zero volatility — the net asset value curve is a near-straight upward line
epargne-liquide.alt_0_pro_1: Exposure to diversified corporate credit risk — outperformance potential
epargne-liquide.alt_0_con_0: Holds physical bonds → 30% Reynders tax on gains at sale
epargne-liquide.alt_0_con_1: Less tax-efficient than XEON for most Belgian investors
epargne-liquide.alt_0_verdict: The gross yield gap (+0.2%) is wiped out by the Reynders tax for almost all Belgian investors. XEON remains preferable.
epargne-liquide.alt_1_con_1: Slightly less liquidity on Belgian markets
epargne-liquide.alt_1_verdict: Direct alternative to XEON with the same €STR mechanism. XEON remains preferable for its superior liquidity on Euronext.
epargne-liquide.warning_0: ⚠️ Yield follows the ECB policy rate — it will fall if the ECB reduces rates.
epargne-liquide.warning_1: ⚠️ The Reynders tax exemption is based on current tax practice, not on a codified law. This treatment could evolve.
epargne-liquide.warning_2: ℹ️ Not suitable as a long-term strategy — for growth, prefer an equity strategy.

savings-ing-tempo.name: ING Savings Account (Tempo Sparen)
savings-ing-tempo.description: Base rate 0.75% + fidelity premium 1.50%. Deposits limited to €500/month. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-kbc.name: KBC Savings Account (Start2Save)
savings-kbc.description: Base rate 0.75% + fidelity premium 1.50%. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-vdk.name: vdk banque Savings Account (Ritme Spaarrekening)
savings-vdk.description: Base rate 1.35% + fidelity premium 1.50%. Among the best rates on the market. Deposits limited to €500/month. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-crelan.name: Crelan Savings Account (GoSave)
savings-crelan.description: Base rate 0.50% + fidelity premium 1.50%. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-keytrade.name: Keytrade Savings Account (High Fidelity)
savings-keytrade.description: Base rate 0.30% + fidelity premium 1.30%. Online bank. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-medirect.name: MeDirect Savings Account (Fidelity Savings)
savings-medirect.description: Base rate 0.20% + fidelity premium 1.40%. Online bank. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

bon-caisse-kbc-1yr.name: KBC Savings Bond (1 year)
bon-caisse-kbc-1yr.description: Gross rate 2.94% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted at source. Min €1,000. Capital locked for 1 year.

bon-caisse-kbc-3yr.name: KBC Savings Bond (3 years)
bon-caisse-kbc-3yr.description: Gross rate 3.64% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted. Capital locked for 3 years. Early withdrawal: significant penalties.

bon-caisse-kbc-5yr.name: KBC Savings Bond (5 years)
bon-caisse-kbc-5yr.description: Gross rate 3.74% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted. Capital locked for 5 years.

bon-caisse-argenta-1yr.name: Argenta Savings Bond (1 year)
bon-caisse-argenta-1yr.description: Gross rate 2.10% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted. Min €250.

bon-caisse-argenta-5yr.name: Argenta Savings Bond (5 years)
bon-caisse-argenta-5yr.description: Gross rate 2.45% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted.

bon-caisse-ing-1yr.name: ING Savings Bond (1 year)
bon-caisse-ing-1yr.description: Gross rate 2.20% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted. Durations of 2–3 years available on request in branch.

bon-caisse-beobank-1yr.name: Beobank Term Account (1 year)
bon-caisse-beobank-1yr.description: Gross rate 3.10% — 30% withholding tax automatically deducted. Capital locked for 1 year.

branche21.name: Branch 21 Life Insurance (AG Insurance)
branche21.description: Guaranteed rate 1.75% + profit sharing. Total return 2025: ~3.10% (not guaranteed). 2% premium tax + ~3% entry fees. 30% withholding tax on fictitious return (4.75%/year) if redeemed before 8 years; exempt after 8 years.

branche21-ethias-9yr.name: Ethias Branch 21 Life Insurance (9 years)
branche21-ethias-9yr.description: Guaranteed rate 2.90% for the entire duration of the contract. 0% entry fees — no management fees. 2% premium tax. Exempt from withholding tax after 8 years.

branche21-ethias-3yr.name: Ethias Branch 21 Life Insurance (3 years)
branche21-ethias-3yr.description: Guaranteed rate 2.30% for the 3-year duration. 0% entry fees. 2% premium tax. This 3-year contract can never reach the 8-year exemption — the 30% fictitious withholding tax always applies at maturity.

bnp-future-invest-bon.name: Future Invest Bond
bnp-future-invest-bon.description: Double rate: 3.00% the 1st year, 2.60% the following 7 years (blended ~2.64%). Global return 2025: ~3.10% (including profit sharing). Distributed by BNP Paribas Fortis, underlying insured by AG Insurance. Fixed duration of 8 years and 1 month. 2% premium tax, ~2% entry fees.

beobank-invest-21.name: Invest 21
beobank-invest-21.description: Guaranteed rate 2.50%. Entry fees up to 2.50% (sometimes reduced to 1.00% during promotional campaigns). Capital guaranteed at maturity excluding fees and taxes. 2% premium tax. Exempt from withholding tax after 8 years.

pension-kbc-pricos.name: KBC Pension Savings (Pricos)
pension-kbc-pricos.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.34% (net of 1.41% TER). Equity-heavy fund. 2% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-belfius-equities.name: Belfius Pension Savings (High Equities, Candriam)
pension-belfius-equities.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.26% (net of 1.49% TER). Equity-heavy fund. 3% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-bnp-growth.name: BNP Paribas Pension Savings (B Pension Sust. Growth)
pension-bnp-growth.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.50% (net of 1.25% TER). 3% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-ing-star.name: ING Pension Savings (Star Fund)
pension-ing-star.description: 10-year annualised return: ~4.72% (net of 1.28% TER). Mixed fund (~40% bonds). 3% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-argenta.name: Argenta Pension Savings (Pension Fund)
pension-argenta.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.31% (net of 1.44% TER). Equity-heavy fund. No entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-vdk.name: vdk banque Pension Savings (Pension Fund)
pension-vdk.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.25% (net of 1.50% TER). Equity-heavy fund, ESG-oriented. 2% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

pension-crelan.name: Crelan Pension Savings (Pension Fund Sust. Growth)
pension-crelan.description: 10-year annualised return: ~5.25% (net of 1.50% TER). Equity-heavy fund with sustainable criteria. 3% entry fees. 30% tax advantage on deposits up to €1,050/year.

fonds-bnp-equity-world.name: BNP Comfort Sust. Equity World Fund
fonds-bnp-equity-world.description: 10-year annualised return (2016–2025), net of 1.50% TER. The fund underperformed its benchmark for most years. 3% entry fees.

fonds-belfius-equity-world.name: Belfius Managed Portfolio Equity World Fund
fonds-belfius-equity-world.description: Annualised return ~6.51% (net of 1.49% TER). 3% entry fees, 1.49% TER.

fonds-axa-belgium-equity.name: AXA B Fund Equity Belgium Fund
fonds-axa-belgium-equity.description: Annualised return ~6.53% (net of 1.47% TER). Exposure only to Belgian equities — high geographical concentration risk. -26% drawdown in 2022. 3% entry fees.

fonds-nagelmackers-growth.name: Nagelmackers MultiFund Growth Fund
fonds-nagelmackers-growth.description: Estimated annualised return ~4.65% (net of 2.10% annual TER). TER among the highest on the market. Mixed fund (~25% bonds, ~75% equities). 2.5% entry fees.

fonds-ing-star-active.name: ING Fund (Star Fund)
fonds-ing-star-active.description: Annualised return ~4.72% (net of 1.28% TER). Mixed fund (~40% bonds, ~60% equities). 3% entry fees.

fonds-beobank-equities.name: Beobank Full Equities Strategy Fund B Fund
fonds-beobank-equities.description: Annualised return ~5.80% (net of 2.20% TER). 100% global equity fund. High TER. 2.5% entry fees.

savings-belfius-flow.name: Belfius Savings Account (Flow)
savings-belfius-flow.description: Base rate 1.30% + fidelity premium 1.50%. Premium acquired after 12 consecutive months without withdrawal. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

savings-argenta.name: Argenta Savings Account (Groeirekening)
savings-argenta.description: Base rate 1.10% + fidelity premium 1.50%. First €1,020 of interest/year exempt from 30% withholding tax.

fiscal.standard: 0.12% TOB on purchase and sale. 10% capital gains tax on annual net gains exceeding the €10,000 allowance — the total gains are taxed at 10% once the threshold is crossed (not just the excess). Unused allowance can be carried forward up to €15,000 over 5 years. Accumulating funds: no annual withholding tax on automatically reinvested dividends. Rates used are indicative — past performance does not guarantee future results.
fiscal.distributing: 0.12% TOB on purchase and sale. 30% withholding tax (précompte mobilier) on each dividend payment. This recurring tax burden significantly reduces the net return compared to an equivalent accumulating ETF. Rates used are indicative — past performance does not guarantee future results.
fiscal.reynders: 0.12% TOB on purchase and sale. 30% Reynders tax on capital gains at sale, applicable because this fund holds more than 10% in bonds. This tax applies to the total gains — no €10,000 exemption. Rates used are indicative — past performance does not guarantee future results.
fiscal.epargne_liquide: 0.12% TOB on purchase and sale. Reynders tax: in principle not applicable (synthetic structure — no direct bond holdings), but this treatment is subject to evolving tax interpretation — to be treated as potentially subject. No annual withholding tax (accumulating fund). The tax treatment of the synthetic structure is based on current Belgian administrative practice — it could evolve if the tax authorities change their doctrine.

comprendre.hero_subtitle: Three clear guides to make the right decisions — no jargon, no conflict of interest.
comprendre.card1_desc: How ETFs structurally beat banking products over the long term — and why fees change everything.
comprendre.card2_desc: Savings account, savings bond, branch 21, pension savings, active funds — how they really work and when they make sense.
comprendre.card3_desc: TOB, withholding tax, Reynders tax, ETF capital gains — everything you need to know to optimise your situation.
comprendre.disclaimer: These guides are provided for educational purposes only and do not constitute investment advice. ClearInvest is not MiFID II authorised.

comprendreEtf.hero_subtitle: The honest answer to the question your bank doesn't want you to ask.
comprendreEtf.s1_p1: An ETF (Exchange Traded Fund) is an investment fund that automatically replicates a stock market index — for example, the 1,400 largest global companies.
comprendreEtf.s2_p1: Instead of betting on a single company, an ETF allows you to own a fraction of hundreds or thousands of companies simultaneously. If one company goes bankrupt, the impact on your portfolio is negligible.
comprendreEtf.s3_p1: Traditional banking funds often charge between 1.5% and 2.5% in annual fees. An ETF like those recommended here costs between 0.07% and 0.20% per year. Over 30 years, this difference in fees can represent more than 30% of your final capital.
comprendreEtf.s3_p2: Management fees are the only variable you can control with certainty. Predicting the future of the market is impossible; reducing your costs is a guaranteed return.
comprendreEtf.s3_p3: The practical conclusion: paying 1.5% in additional fees for active management is paying for a promise that statistics prove is rarely kept over the long term.
comprendreEtf.s4_title: Why banking products can't keep up
comprendreEtf.s4_p1: Classic savings products suffer from three structural handicaps compared to ETFs:
comprendreEtf.h1_body: A savings account at 2.5% in a 2% inflation environment earns you 0.5% real. Your purchasing power stagnates. Over 20 years, your capital does not double — it loses value in real terms as soon as inflation exceeds your return.
comprendreEtf.h2_body: Interest from savings bonds and term accounts is taxed at 30% from the first cent. A gross rate of 3.10% becomes 2.17% net. Accumulating ETFs, on the other hand, generate no taxable income until you sell — gains compound freely for decades.
comprendreEtf.h3_body: A savings account cannot make you rich. Its return is capped by ECB policy rates, redistributed with a generous margin for the bank. In 2015, the best savings accounts earned 0.50%. In 2022, some fell to 0.01%. A global ETF follows global economic growth — a structurally more powerful engine.
comprendreEtf.s5_p1: That would be false. Banking products have their place — but a precise and limited place.
comprendreEtf.s5_p2: A savings account is irreplaceable for your emergency fund: 3 to 6 months of expenses, available immediately, without risk of capital loss. This is its legitimate function.
comprendreEtf.s5_p3: Beyond this security reserve, every euro left indefinitely in a savings account is a euro that underperforms.
comprendreEtf.s5_quote: The question is not 'savings or investment?' It's 'what part of my savings needs to be liquid and risk-free?'
comprendreEtf.s6_title: The Belgian context — why it's even truer here
comprendreEtf.s6_p1: Belgium presents a particularly unfavourable combination for the passive saver:
comprendreEtf.s6_bullet1: The four major banks (BNP Paribas Fortis, KBC, Belfius, ING) control the majority of deposits and pass on rate increases slowly and partially to savings accounts
comprendreEtf.s6_bullet2: Pension savings funds show TERs of 1.25% to 1.50%, among the highest in Europe for this type of product
comprendreEtf.s6_bullet3: Stock exchange tax (TOB) is 0.12% for accumulating ETFs domiciled outside Belgium (in the EEA) — which covers almost all ETFs recommended here. For funds registered in Belgium, it rises to 1.32%, which significantly changes the game.
comprendreEtf.s6_bullet4: Since 1 January 2026, capital gains on equity ETFs are subject to a 10% tax beyond an annual allowance of €10,000 per taxpayer. This is a moderate burden compared to many neighbouring countries, and the first €10,000 of annual gains remain exempt.
comprendreEtf.cta_subtitle: Compare ETF strategies adapted to the Belgian context, or simulate your situation with our calculator.
comprendreEtf.s1_p2: Concretely, buying a share of IMIE means buying a fraction of Apple, Microsoft, Nestlé, Samsung, Toyota and 8,999 other companies in a single transaction. Your capital is instantly diversified on a global scale.
comprendreEtf.s1_p3: Two characteristics distinguish ETFs from traditional funds:
comprendreEtf.s1_bullet1: They are tradable on the stock exchange like an ordinary share
comprendreEtf.s1_bullet2: Their annual fees are tiny: between 0.07% and 0.20% per year, compared to 1.20% to 2.50% for an actively managed fund
comprendreEtf.sim_note: Indicative simulation before taxation. Identical gross rate — only fees differ.
comprendreEtf.s2_p2: It's not a question of the manager's talent. It's a mathematical question. Over 30 years, every euro taken in fees is a euro that no longer compounds. The effect is exponential: fees don't just reduce your return by 1.5% — they reduce your final capital by 25 to 35%.

comprFiscalite.hero_subtitle: Six taxes to know — and how to minimise them legally.
comprFiscalite.intro: Since 1 January 2026, Belgium has introduced a 10% tax on financial capital gains beyond an annual allowance of €10,000 per taxpayer. Despite this change, the Belgian tax regime remains favourable to the passive investor: several taxes simply do not apply to accumulating equity ETFs. Understanding these rules allows you to optimise your strategy legally.
comprFiscalite.s1_p1: Each tax has its specific trigger. Understanding which ones apply to each product allows you to anticipate the real tax cost of your investment.
comprFiscalite.tax1_applies: Each purchase AND each sale of an ETF or fund
comprFiscalite.tax1_when: Automatically deducted by the Belgian broker, or to be declared yourself with a foreign broker
comprFiscalite.tax1_insight: The rate depends on two factors: an accumulating ETF not registered in Belgium (IMIE, IWDA, CSPX, CNDX, XMAW, XEON) is at 0.12%. An accumulating ETF registered in Belgium (e.g. VWCE) is at 1.32% — the main reason we advise against it. Distributing ETFs are always at 0.12%, regardless of their registration, but trigger the 30% withholding tax on dividends. For Belgian investors, an accumulating ETF not registered in Belgium is optimal on both fronts.
comprFiscalite.tax2_applies: Dividends, interest from term accounts and savings bonds, interest from regulated savings accounts beyond the exemption ceiling (taxed at 15%, a specific feature of Belgian regulated accounts)
comprFiscalite.tax2_when: Withheld at source by the financial institution
comprFiscalite.tax2_insight: This is the main reason to prefer accumulating ETFs over distributing ones: an accumulating ETF automatically reinvests its dividends without triggering withholding tax. Your capital compounds freely.
comprFiscalite.tax3_applies: Capitalisation funds whose portfolio contains more than 10% bonds or cash
comprFiscalite.tax3_insight: The Reynders tax strikes only the fraction of the capital gain attributable to the bond component of the fund — not the entire gain. A 60% equity / 40% bond fund sees only the bond portion of its gain taxed at 30%. Purely equity ETFs (IMIE, IWDA, CSPX, CNDX, XMAW) do not contain any and escape this tax entirely. XEON (synthetic money market): uncertain tax status — to be treated as potentially subject to Reynders tax.
comprFiscalite.tax4_applies: Capital gains realised on the sale of equities and equity ETFs
comprFiscalite.tax4_when: Upon sale, via the broker or tax declaration
comprFiscalite.tax4_insight: The first €10,000 of annual capital gains remain exempt. Unused allowance can be carried forward: up to €1,000 per year for a maximum of 5 years, making a cumulative allowance of up to €15,000. A strategy of progressive sales over several years allows for optimising this allowance.
comprFiscalite.tax5_applies: Capital accumulated in a pension savings fund (branch 23)
comprFiscalite.tax5_insight: The tax is calculated on a fictitious capital, as if your fund had earned 4.75% per year since the first payment — regardless of actual performance. If your fund earned less than 4.75%, you proportionally pay more tax on your real return. This rule applies only to bank pension savings funds (investment funds, branch 23). For insurance products (branch 21), the 8% tax is calculated on the capital actually built up.
comprFiscalite.tax6_applies: Each payment into a branch 21 savings-insurance contract
comprFiscalite.tax6_when: Deducted immediately from each premium paid, before investment
comprFiscalite.tax6_insight: Out of €10,000 paid, only €9,800 is actually invested. This tax is irreversible and applies even if you redeem the contract shortly after.
comprFiscalite.s2_p1: An overview of applicable taxes by product type. Rows in green correspond to ETFs — their tax profile is structurally more favourable.
comprFiscalite.table_note1: * TOB: for accumulating ETFs. Distributing ETFs are always at 0.12%, regardless of their registration.
comprFiscalite.table_note2: ** Reynders: 30% only on the bond component of the capital gain — not on the entire gain.
comprFiscalite.disclaimer_text: This page presents the taxation in force in 2026 for informational purposes. Tax legislation may evolve — we update this content accordingly.

comprFiscalite.cta_subtitle: Our calculator integrates each tax described on this page — TOB, withholding tax, Reynders, CGT — to give you the real net return.
