comprProduits.hero_subtitle: What your bank offers you — and what it doesn't always tell you.
comprProduits.intro: Banking products are not all bad. Some fulfill a useful function. But understanding exactly how they work — their real fees, their taxation, their constraints — is essential for making an informed decision.
comprProduits.s1_p1: The Belgian savings account works with two components: a base rate (paid from the first day) and a fidelity premium (acquired only if the capital remains on the account for 12 consecutive months).
comprProduits.s1_p2: The fidelity premium is a behavioural mechanism: it penalises unforeseen withdrawals and secures low-cost financing for the bank. If you withdraw before 12 months, you lose the premium on the amount withdrawn.
comprProduits.s1_fiscal_text: The first €1,020 of annual interest per person (amount for 2026 income, indexed annually) is exempt from withholding tax. Beyond that, each additional euro of interest is taxed at 15% — and not 30% like other mobile income: this is one of the rare tax advantages of the regulated savings account.
comprProduits.s1_verdict_p1: The savings account has a precise and legitimate function: your emergency fund (3 to 6 months of expenses), available at any time without risk of capital loss.
comprProduits.s1_verdict_p2: For this function, it is irreplaceable. For everything else — medium or long-term savings — every year spent in a savings account is a year where your purchasing power stagnates or retreats against inflation.
comprProduits.s2_title: The savings bond and term account
comprProduits.s2_p1: These products lock your capital for a fixed duration (1 to 10 years) in exchange for a guaranteed rate. The certainty of the return is paid for in two ways:
comprProduits.s2_pt1_body: Unlike the savings account, all interest is subject to 30% withholding tax from the first cent. A displayed gross rate of 3.10% becomes 2.17% net.
comprProduits.s2_pt2_body: Early withdrawal is generally impossible or penalised. Your capital is locked until the contractual maturity.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p1: In almost all cases, a savings bond or term account is inferior to a simpler alternative.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p2: After the 30% withholding tax, a 3.10% gross rate becomes 2.17% net — all while locking your capital. A money market ETF like XEON offers a return close to the ECB policy rate — generally higher than the best term accounts after tax, while remaining totally liquid and without triggering annual withholding tax.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p3: The only argument for a savings bond would be not wanting to open an account with a broker. In this case, a high-rate savings account (vdk Rythme, Belfius Flow) remains more flexible for the same risk profile.
comprProduits.s3_title_sub: (savings-insurance, not a banking product in the strict sense)
comprProduits.s3_p1: Branch 21 is technically a life insurance contract offered by insurance companies, not a bank deposit — even if banks commonly distribute it. It offers a guaranteed interest rate, supplemented by a possible non-guaranteed profit sharing. Its taxation is specific:
comprProduits.s3_bullet1: A 2% premium tax is levied on each payment before any investment
comprProduits.s3_bullet2: If you redeem before 8 years, a 30% withholding tax applies to interest — except in case of the insured's death (130% clause), which exempts from withholding regardless of the duration
comprProduits.s3_bullet3: After 8 years, interest is exempt from withholding tax
comprProduits.s3_warning: The 8-year rule trap: a product presented as 'secure' actually locks your capital for 8 years to be tax-optimal. And the 2% tax on each premium immediately reduces your invested capital.
comprProduits.s3_example_text: Out of €10,000 paid, only €9,800 is invested (after 2% tax). At a guaranteed rate of 2.50%, it takes about a year just to recover this initial tax.
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p1: Branch 21 is structurally disadvantageous for the ordinary investor:
comprProduits.s3_b1: 2% premium tax deducted immediately from each payment
comprProduits.s3_b2: Capital locked for 8 years to avoid 30% withholding
comprProduits.s3_b3: Guaranteed rate of 2.50% to 3.10% — barely higher than the best savings accounts, without the liquidity
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p2: On a short horizon (<8 years), XEON is more profitable and liquid. On a long horizon (>8 years), an equity ETF structurally surpasses this guaranteed return.
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p3: Branch 21 wins on no horizon for an investor who accepts using a broker.
comprProduits.s4_title: Pension savings — the myth of the tax advantage
comprProduits.s4_p1: Pension savings appeal through their immediate tax advantage, available in two options:
comprProduits.s4_opt1_text: Up to €1,050/year paid → 30% tax reduction → up to €315 recovered.
comprProduits.s4_opt2_text: Up to €1,350/year paid → 25% tax reduction → up to €337 recovered.
comprProduits.s4_threshold: Tipping point: the increased option only becomes advantageous from €1,260/year in payments. Below this, the standard option is preferable.
comprProduits.s4_p2: But three elements temper this enthusiasm:
comprProduits.s4_pt1_body: Belgian pension savings funds show TERs of 1.25% to 1.50%, to which are added entry fees of 0% to 3% depending on the institution. Over 30 years, these fees can erase a significant part of the initial tax advantage.
comprProduits.s4_pt2_title: The exit tax — on a fictitious capital
comprProduits.s4_pt2_body: At age 60, the state levies an anticipatory tax of 8% — not on your real capital, but on a fictitious capital calculated as if your fund (branch 23) had earned 4.75% per year from the start. This fictitious calculation applies to branch 23 pension savings funds only; branch 21 follows a different mechanism. If your fund underperformed the 4.75% threshold, your effective tax rate on the real return increases considerably.
comprProduits.s4_pt3_body: Capital is locked until age 60. Early withdrawal triggers taxation at the personal income tax (PIT) rate of 33%, to which are added municipal surcharges — this is not a withholding tax, but a final tax that can exceed 35% depending on the municipality of residence.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p1: The 30% tax advantage seems considerable — but it is a one-time bonus, paid once a year, that does not compound.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p2: Meanwhile, the performance gap between the pension fund (~4%) and an ETF (~8%) compounds every year for decades.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p3: Our simulator demonstrates it concretely: over 30 years at €87.50/month (tax ceiling), an All-in-one ETF surpasses Argenta pension savings from the 11th year — and ends with €33,000 more at 30 years, despite the best fund on the market (0% entry fees) and the maximum tax advantage.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p4: Added to this: capital locked until 60, an exit tax of 8%, and annual management fees of 1.25% to 1.50%.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p5: Our conclusion: pension savings are not an investment strategy — they are a tax product that structurally underperforms over the long term. Keep your capital free and invest it in an ETF.
comprProduits.s5_title: Active funds — why they cost so much
comprProduits.s5_p1: Active mutual funds are the most lucrative product for banks — and often the least advantageous for the investor.
comprProduits.s5_p2: Their economic model relies on retrocessions: the management company levies a TER of 1.50% to 2.50%, part of which is paid back to the distributing bank to remunerate its commercial effort. MiFID II (2018) imposed more transparency — banks must now publish a KID/DICI document mentioning total costs — and banned retrocessions for independent advisors. But traditional banking networks, which operate as tied distributors, can still receive these commissions. In practice, the network client remains exposed to a structural selection bias: recommended funds are those that generate the most retrocessions, not necessarily those that offer the best net return for the investor.
comprProduits.s5_quote: Over 20 years, between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark index after fees. This is not a question of incompetence — it is a mathematical reality linked to the cost of active management.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p1: There is no general case where a bank active fund is the best choice for a long-term investor.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p2: Between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark index over 20 years, after fees. This is not an opinion — it is the documented result of decades of academic studies and Morningstar data.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p3: An ETF with 0.17% TER versus an active fund at 1.73%: the difference seems small. Over 30 years, it often represents more than 30% of the final capital.

immobilierVsEtf.hero_subtitle: The honest comparison that nobody will give you at your bank.
immobilierVsEtf.intro_p1: Belgium has a special relationship with bricks. « Een steen in de maag » — a brick in the stomach — the expression alone summarizes a deep cultural conviction: bricks and mortar are safe, concrete, and real.
immobilierVsEtf.intro_p2: But is it really the best investment for you? The honest answer is: it depends. Not on the markets, not on interest rates — but on what you are willing to put in besides your money.
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p1: With equal capital and no loan, Belgian residential real estate has historically delivered a total return of 6% to 8% per year: 3% to 4% property value appreciation, plus 3% to 4% net rental yield (after charges, vacancies, and maintenance).
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p2: A global ETF like IMIE has historically delivered 7% to 9% per year over the long term.
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p3: The observation: with identical capital and no leverage, both strategies offer comparable returns. The fundamental difference lies elsewhere — in concentration risk, liquidity, and the time you dedicate to it.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_title: The superpower of real estate: leverage
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p1: This is the strongest argument in favor of real estate — and the only one that ETFs cannot easily replicate.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p2: A bank will never lend you €200,000 to buy ETFs. It will do so for bricks.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_example_p1: You buy a €250,000 apartment with a €50,000 deposit. You receive 100% of the rent and capital gain on a €250,000 property, while only having mobilized €50,000. A 6% return on €250,000 represents €15,000/year — which is a 30% return on your initial €50,000 stake.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p3: This is where real estate mathematically beats ETFs: not in the property's yield, but in the amplification by bank leverage.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p4: The flip side: leverage amplifies gains in both directions. An apartment that loses 20% in value with an 80% loan wipes out your entire deposit.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_title: Structural advantages of Belgian real estate
immobilierVsEtf.s3_intro: Belgium offers a tax framework that has historically been favorable to private landlords:
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item1_title: Taxation on cadastral income, not real rents:
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item1_p: If you rent to an individual for private use, you are not taxed on the actual rents received, but on the indexed Cadastral Income (CI) increased by 40% — often much lower than the economic reality.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item2_p: In Belgium, the capital gain on the resale of a property held for more than 5 years is not taxed (in normal management). This is a considerable advantage compared to many neighboring countries.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item3_p: Belgian leases allow for annual indexation of rents based on the health index. The value of bricks and mortar structurally follows inflation over the long term.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item4_p: A property cannot be sold on impulse in 30 seconds. This illiquidity — often presented as a flaw — paradoxically protects the investor against their own reactions during crises.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_title: What real estate really costs you
immobilierVsEtf.s4_intro: The frictions associated with real estate are massive and often underestimated.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item1_p1: In Belgium, for an investment property (excluding own residence), registration duties amount to 12% in Flanders, 12.5% in Brussels and Wallonia. With notary fees and mortgage deed costs, you lose about 15% of the property value on the day of purchase. On €250,000, that's €37,500 gone before the property even generates a single euro.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item1_p2: With an ETF: 0.12% TOB. On €250,000, that's €300.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item2_p: If you need €10,000 urgently, you cannot sell the toilet in your apartment. With an ETF, you sell exactly what you need, and the money is available in 48 hours.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item3_p: Rental real estate is not a passive income. Managing tenants, vacancies, water leaks at 10 PM, general meetings of co-owners, energy standards compliance (mandatory PEB), building manager. It's a second part-time job. An ETF requires 5 minutes a month.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item4_p: Annual property tax, fire insurance, non-recoverable co-ownership charges, provisions for major works — these costs silently eat away at the gross return.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_warning: The gross rental yield displayed by real estate agencies systematically omits these fees. A 5% gross yield can become 2% to 3% net once all real costs are taken into account.
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p1: These are two different strategies aimed at different profiles — not direct competitors.
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_title: Real estate is for you if:
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li1: You want to use your borrowing capacity to amplify your returns via leverage
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li2: You are willing to dedicate time and energy to it (selection, management, maintenance)
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li3: You already have a solid passive investment base and are looking to diversify
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li1: You want to invest without dedicating your free time to it
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li2: You are a beginner and do not yet have a €50,000 deposit
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li4: You want immediate global diversification with any amount
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p2: The real question is not « real estate or ETF? » — it's « do I have the capital, the time, and the appetite to manage a property? »
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p3: If the answer is yes to all three: real estate with leverage can be an excellent complementary strategy. If not: ETFs do the job without friction, without stress, and with comparable returns.
immobilierVsEtf.comp_left_levier: Leverage possible via credit (amplifier depending on yield/rate spread)
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_title: The Interest Rate — the Variable That Changes Everything
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p1: Leverage is only beneficial if your net return exceeds your borrowing cost. This is known as the positive leverage effect.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_rule_pos: If net return on property > mortgage rate → leverage amplifies your gains ✅
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_rule_neg: If net return on property < mortgage rate → leverage destroys value ❌
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_scenario_a_content: Net return on property: 5% | Mortgage rate: 1.5% (2018-2021) | Spread: +3.5% | Result: every borrowed euro earns 3.5 cents more than it costs ✅
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_scenario_b_content: Net return on property: 3% | Mortgage rate: 3.5% (2024-2026) | Spread: -0.5% | Result: every borrowed euro costs 0.5 cents more than it earns ❌ | In this case, you are betting solely on the future capital gain of the property.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p2: Between 2015 and 2021, Belgian mortgage rates oscillated between 1% and 2%. Real estate leverage was then exceptionally powerful — borrowing at 1.5% to generate 5% net represents a spread of 3.5 points.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p3: Since 2022, rates have risen sharply. In 2026, a Belgian mortgage is negotiated between 3% and 4% depending on the profile. With net rental yields often between 2.5% and 3.5% in major Belgian cities, the spread has become neutral or even negative.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p4: This reversal doesn't mean real estate is a bad idea — but it means the thesis of « magic leverage that always beats ETFs » is much less obvious than it was 5 years ago.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_tip: Before any rental real estate project, calculate your real net return (rent - charges - vacancies - maintenance - property tax) and compare it to your credit rate. If the spread is less than 1%, you are essentially betting on the future capital gain of the property — not on the cash flow.
